function_explanation: Computes the Cole-Cole complex resistivity forward model, which describes frequency-dependent electrical resistivity in geophysical applications.
function_docstring:
Computes the Cole-Cole complex resistivity forward model.

This function calculates the complex resistivity ρ*(ω) using the Cole-Cole model:
ρ*(ω) = ρ_0 × [1 - m × (1 - 1/(1 + (iωτ)^c))]

The Cole-Cole model is commonly used in spectral induced polarization (SIP)
measurements to characterize the frequency-dependent electrical properties
of geological materials.

Args:
    freq: A numpy array of frequencies in Hz at which to evaluate the model.
        Can be any shape, typically 1-D with N frequency points.
    rho0: DC resistivity in Ohm·m. This is the resistivity at zero frequency.
    m: Chargeability parameter, dimensionless, typically in range [0, 1].
        Represents the magnitude of the polarization effect.
    tau: Time constant in seconds. Characterizes the relaxation time of
        the polarization process.
    c: Frequency exponent, dimensionless, typically in range [0, 1].
        Controls the broadness of the relaxation time distribution.

Returns:
    Complex numpy array with the same shape as freq, containing the
    complex resistivity values at each frequency. The real part represents
    the in-phase resistivity and the imaginary part represents the
    out-of-phase (quadrature) resistivity.
usage_example:
import numpy as np
from agent_forward_operator import forward_operator

# Define frequency range (log-spaced from 0.001 Hz to 1000 Hz)
freq = np.logspace(-3, 3, 50)

# Cole-Cole model parameters
rho0 = 100.0  # DC resistivity (Ohm·m)
m = 0.3       # Chargeability
tau = 0.1     # Time constant (s)
c = 0.5       # Frequency exponent

# Compute complex resistivity
rho_star = forward_operator(freq, rho0, m, tau, c)

# Extract real and imaginary parts
rho_real = np.real(rho_star)
rho_imag = np.imag(rho_star)

print(f"Complex resistivity shape: {rho_star.shape}")
print(f"Real part range: {rho_real.min():.2f} to {rho_real.max():.2f} Ohm·m")
print(f"Imaginary part range: {rho_imag.min():.2f} to {rho_imag.max():.2f} Ohm·m")
